coredata.dc:title: Theranostic nanozyme: Silk fibroin based multifunctional nanocomposites to combat oxidative stress
coredata.dc:creator: Passi, Mehak
Kumar, Vinay
Packirisamy, Gopinath
coredata.dc:description: Multifunctional nanomaterials integrating therapeutic and imaging modalities in one platform have opened a new era in the present therapeutic strategies. In the present study, a multifunctional silk fibroin-based carrier has been designed for the delivery of antioxidant and imaging agents. One-step desolvation method was used to prepare sulforaphane (antioxidant drug) loaded silk fibroin nanoparticles (SFSNPs). These anionic SFSNPs were further coupled with cationic cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeNPs) and PEI passivated carbon dots (CDs) to form self-assembled CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites. CDs were synthesized from mulberry leaves (Morus indica) as green source of carbon and bPEI as a passivating agent to get positively charged CDs. The CDs functioned as molecular probes by emitting green fluorescence while the presence of CeNPs augmented the antioxidant potential due to their unique redox property. Time-dependent in vitro release of sulforaphane was fast in acidic pH than under normal physiological conditions. Cytotoxicity studies were performed on L132 normal epithelial lung cell lines and A549 lung cancer cell lines to analyze the toxicity of the nanocomposites. Green fluorescence from the CDs facilitated in fluorescence microscopic imaging and cellular uptake studies. ROS scavenging capability was analyzed by exposing cells to H2O2 stress using flow cytometry and DCFH-DA staining. Overall, the synthesized CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites efficiently reduced ROS levels by simultaneously enabling imaging of the cells. Thus, this CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposite could be a potential candidate for simultaneous imaging and drug delivery against oxidative stress.

head.ce:title: Theranostic nanozyme: Silk fibroin based multifunctional nanocomposites to combat oxidative stress

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional nanomaterials integrating therapeutic and imaging modalities in one platform have opened a new era in the present therapeutic strategies. In the present study, a multifunctional silk fibroin-based carrier has been designed for the delivery of antioxidant and imaging agents. One-step desolvation method was used to prepare sulforaphane (antioxidant drug) loaded silk fibroin nanoparticles (SFSNPs). These anionic SFSNPs were further coupled with cationic cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeNPs) and PEI passivated carbon dots (CDs) to form self-assembled CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites. CDs were synthesized from mulberry leaves (Morus indica) as green source of carbon and bPEI as a passivating agent to get positively charged CDs. The CDs functioned as molecular probes by emitting green fluorescence while the presence of CeNPs augmented the antioxidant potential due to their unique redox property. Time-dependent in vitro release of sulforaphane was fast in acidic pH than under normal physiological conditions. Cytotoxicity studies were performed on L132 normal epithelial lung cell lines and A549 lung cancer cell lines to analyze the toxicity of the nanocomposites. Green fluorescence from the CDs facilitated in fluorescence microscopic imaging and cellular uptake studies. ROS scavenging capability was analyzed by exposing cells to H2O2 stress using flow cytometry and DCFH-DA staining. Overall, the synthesized CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites efficiently reduced ROS levels by simultaneously enabling imaging of the cells. Thus, this CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposite could be a potential candidate for simultaneous imaging and drug delivery against oxidative stress.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxidative stress is one of the major causes of the pathogenesis of various disorders such as Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), lung cancer, cystic fibrosis, Alzheimer, Parkinson and cardiovascular dysfunctions. It arises due to the generation of harmful reactive oxygen species (ROS) and the body's inability to detoxify them completely. Various ROS such as superoxide anions, hydroxyl radicals, hydrogen peroxides are either produced endogenously from aerobic metabolic reactions or are derived from exogenous sources such as air pollutants, cigarette smoke etc. [1]. These ROS react with various biological macromolecules causing oxidation of DNA, lipids and proteins thereby inducing secondary cellular responses which are responsible for the progression of diseases such as lung cancer, COPD, asthma and cystic fibrosis [2,3]. Conventional antioxidant therapies have several drawbacks associated with them such as the generation of secondary ROS, affects other organs and tissues [4]. Till now various approaches have been applied to tackle and safeguard the cells from oxidative damage and restore the natural antioxidant activities but no approach has proven to be satisfactory. There are various new strategies that can prove to be advantageous in antioxidant therapy of these diseases such as gene based cell engineering approaches, liposomal based oral and aerosol drug delivery, nanotechnology based approaches combining diagnostics and therapeutics together such as theranostics [5–8]. This provokes the need to develop an antioxidant enzyme mimetic system that is safe, biocompatible and have a high ROS scavenging activity.

Silk is a naturally occurring, FDA approved protein-based biopolymer derived from B.mori silkworms. It has been used as suture materials since ages and for the fabrication of 3D scaffolds, films, fibres, microshperers, nanoparticles, sponges etc. Silk consists of two proteins silk fibroin and silk sericin. Silk fibroin is major hydrophobic fibrous protein while silk sericin is the hydrophilic glue like protein surrounding it [9]. Silk fibroin exhibits excellent mechanical properties, tunable biodegradability, and exceptional biocompatibility and demonstrates pH-dependent drug release [10,11]. Silk fibroin has various advantages such as a carrier for loading of sensitive drugs and therapeutic biomolecules such as proteins and nucleic acid using a mild process to provide good resistance to dissolution, enzymatic and thermal degradation. These proteins contain a vast range of amino acids having functional groups including alcohols, amines, phenols, thiols and carboxyl groups that allows the attachment of various therapeutic biomolecules or antibodies for specific types of cells. Other important natural properties of silk fibroin is that they have an inherent capability to respond as a pH dependent drug release. Most likely degradation mechanism of silk fibroin by the proteolytic enzymes and absorption to eliminate from the body without causing any side effects. The mechanism involved during the release and loading of the drug or biomolecule from the silk fibroin is the electrostatic interaction because silk fibroin posses negatively charge (−24 mV to −26mV) which provides opportunities for a strong electrostatic force between positively charged drugs as compared to negatively charged drugs. So, these strong electrostatic interactions could prevent the significant burst release as normally seen in various polymeric nanocarriers [12]. All these properties make silk fibroin an excellent biomaterials for tissue engineering applications and a potential drug delivery carrier in the field of nanomedicine [13–16]. Several methods have been adopted for fabrication of silk fibroin nanoparticles and microparticles of various sizes including capillary microdot technique (∼100 nm) [17] supercritical fluid CO2 technology (∼50 nm) [18], salting out method (500–1000 nm) [19], desolvation method (50–150 nm) [20], phase separation (500 nm −2 mm) [21], and electrospraying (80 nm) [22].

Carbon dots are a newly discovered class of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial's possessing size of less than 10 nm. They display versatile properties such as ease of fabrication, high solubility, chemical inertness, prolonged luminescence, low toxicity and easy functionalization as compared to conventional dyes and semiconductor quantum dots [23]. Thus, they are an alluring candidate for bio-imaging applications. Many green sources such as coriander leaves [24], green tea [25], garlic [26], potato [27], banana [28], rose flowers [29], Catharanthus roseus [30], aloe [31], silk coccons [32] and rice husks [33] have been utilized for the preparation of carbon dots. Mulberry leaves are natural antioxidants and are a rich source of vitamins, proteins and abundant in carbon, nitrogen and oxygen [34]. They contain a compound named Quercetin that is a natural antioxidant. It shows stability at higher temperatures and possesses a melting point of 316 °C [35]. PEI-passivated carbon dots were synthesized from mulberry leaves using a single-step green hydrothermal approach as previously reported by our group [36]. These bright biocompatible CD-PEI could illuminate cells with green fluorescence.

Sulforaphane (1-isothiocyanate-4- (methyl sulfinyl)-butane) is an organosulfur compound consisting of isothiocyanate group found in cruciferous vegetables such as broccoli, cauliflower and kale. It is widely known for its antioxidant defence mechanism comprising activation of detoxifying enzymatic genes such as superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase and Quinone reductase via Nrf2 pathway of antioxidant response element (ARE) [37,38]. It is therefore known to prevent oxidative stress. However, poor water solubility, sensitivity to oxygen, heat and alkaline conditions restrain the use of sulforaphane against oxidative stress. Encapsulation of sulforaphane within a natural polymer such as silk fibroin can tailor its solubility and enhance its bioavailability against ROS.

Over the years catalytic particles such as cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeNPs) have emerged as potential ROS scavenging candidates. They demonstrate antioxidant enzyme mimetic activity and possess high biocompatibility, good stability, no storage problems and low production cost. The antioxidant activity of the CeNPs lies within its potential to have an oxygen defect and mixed valency state i.e. oxidized (+4) and reduced (+3) oxidized state enabling them with superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase mimetic activity [39–41]. Apart from all these properties, still there is a need to address many unresolved challenges with its use such as short retention time, poor solubility and use of harmful chemicals in its production [42]. This urges for the need to develop a stable method for delivery of CeNPs overcoming the obstacles with its use. The present study deals with developing a multifunctional theranostic antioxidant delivery system that successfully removes the ROS and simultaneously enabling the intracellular imaging. CeNP-CD@SFSNPs self-assembled nanocomposites were initially prepared by a single step desolvation method of silk fibroin and entrapment of D, l-sulforaphane drug within the synthesized nanoparticles. It is followed by non-covalent complexation of CeNPs and CDs, on the surface of the nanoparticles that involved electrostatic interactions between negatively charged SFSNPs and positively charged CeNPs and CDs. To the best of our knowledge, it is the first time that a multifunctional nanocomposite with imaging and antioxidant activities have been reported where CDs and CeNPs have been coupled together with sulforaphane-loaded silk fibroin nanoparticles. CDs were used to visualize the intracellular uptake of the drug entrapped silk fibroin nanoparticles whereas CeNPs enhanced their antioxidant efficiency (See Scheme 1 ).

B.mori cocoons were procured from Central silk board Dehradun, Uttarakhand, India. D, l-Sulforaphane ≥90%, CAS Number- 4478-93-7 (HPLC), ammonium cerium (IV) nitrate ((NH4)2Ce(NO3)6) were purchased, from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Fresh Mulberry leaves were purchased locally. Dialysis membrane 150(MWCO-12-14 kDa), bPEI (Mw −25000), lithium bromide, sodium carbonate, sodium bicarbonate was acquired from HiMedia, India. Sodium acetate (CH3COONa), acetone, acetic acid, Dimethyl sulfoxide acid (DMSO) and Ethanol ( SRL Chemicals Pvt Ltd, India), Quinine sulphate ( Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd, India.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isolation of silk fibroin (SF) solution from B.mori cocoons
ce:section.ce:para: Silk fibroin solution was isolated by the previously reported degumming method with minor modification [43]. Initially, 5 g of B.mori silk cocoons were boiled in 2L of 0.2%(w/v) Na2CO3 solution for 1 h followed by its dissolution in 9.4 M LiBr solution for 4 h at 60 °C in hot air oven. After that LiBr dissolved silk fibres was dialyzed against distilled water for 4 days at room temperature using dialysis membrane (MWCO 12-14 kDa) to obtain a tinted yellow regenerated silk fibroin solution of 5% w/v.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of sulforaphane encapsulated silk fibroin nanoparticles (SFSNPs)
ce:section.ce:para:
D, l-Sulforaphane loaded silk fibroin nanoparticles were synthesized in aqueous solution by adding acetone to 5% w/v SF solution [44]. Briefly, 1 mg mL−1 of D, l-Sulforaphane solution was drop wise added to 5% w/v silk fibroin solution. The solution was stirred for 30 min followed by dropwise addition of 85% (v/v) acetone solution and allowed to stir overnight. The milky precipitates (as SFSNPs) was observed after 12 h stirring and this precipitates were recovered by centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 30 min. These nanoparticles were further dispersed in deionized water and sonicated using Qsonica Q500 probe sonicator at an amplitude of 30% for 2 s ON-OFF pulse. Further, for indirect determination of sulforaphane entrapment efficiency, the supernatants were analyzed spectrophotometrically at 254 nm using an ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer. The entrapment efficiency of sulforaphane was calculated as follows:
EE% =

( Weight of free sulforaphane in the supernatent )

EE% = Percentage entrapment efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of bPEI- passivated carbon dots (CDs) from mulberry leaves
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence emitting carbon dots were synthesized by modifying previously reported hydrothermal method using mulberry leaves (Morus indica) are the green source of carbon and bPEI as a surface passivating agent to create a positive charge on the surface [36]. Briefly, 0.4 g of bPEI was dissolved in 60 mL of deionized water followed by addition of preciously chopped 5 g of mulberry leaves. The solution was allowed to stir at room temperature for 1 h until it is uniformly mixed. After that, it was shifted to the hydrothermal reaction chamber where it was heated at 200 °C for 4 h. Upon cooling the solution was filtered using Whatman filter paper followed by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 20 min to remove the impurities from the pure CDs. The concentration of purified CDs was adjusted to 10 mg mL−1 with deionized water for further use and characterizations.

Cerium oxide nanoparticles were also synthesized using hydrothermal oxidation method reported previously [45]. Briefly, 2.72 g of ammonium cerium nitrate (0.07 M) and 10 g of sodium acetate were added in 70 mL of ultrapure water followed by addition of 10 mL acetic acid. The solution was then allowed to stir for 2 h to yield a homogenous greenish-yellow solution. After that, it was subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 220 °C for 12 h. Thus, after 12 h reaction brown color precipitate were recovered by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 10 min. Further, several time washing was done with deionized water and ethanol later, these nanoparticles were kept for drying at 60 °C for overnight to form a brown coloured powder as cerium oxide nanoparticles.

The CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites were prepared by conjugating the anionic SFSNPs with amine groups of the CDs and cationic CeNPs electrostatically. Briefly, synthesized bPEI-passivated carbon dots (CDs)(having concentration 10 mg mL−1)were mixed with cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeNPs) (having concentration 5 mg mL−1) with a final concentration of 2:1 ratio in water. Further, the above solution was added to SFSNPs suspension with an initial concentration of 4 mg mL−1 followed by incubation for 12 h. The obtained solution was then syringe filtered to remove the unbound CDs and CeNPs to yield brown-yellowish CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free radical scavenging DPPH assay of CDs and CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: CDs having concentration (20,40,60,80 and 100 μg mL-1 ) were added to 0.5 mL of 50 μM methanolic DPPH solution to make up the final volume to 0.6 mL followed by the incubation for 30 min in a dark environment at 37 °C. After that 100 μL of each sample was added to the 96-well plate. The difference in absorbance was recorded at 517 nm by a multi-mode microplate reader. The DPPH of CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites was also done in a similar manner.

The free radical scavenging activity (%) of CDs was calculated by the following formula:

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro release of sulforaphane from SFSNPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The release of sulforaphane from SFSNPs was measured in acetate buffer (20 mM, pH 4.5) and phosphate-buffered saline (10 mM, pH 7) using SLIDE-LYZER™ MINI dialysis device (10 kDa MWCO). Further, pH based stability of nanocomposites was also analysed. Briefly, 2 mL of SFSNPs suspension was added to dialysis tube followed by addition of 45 mL buffer in the outer compartment. The dialysis device was constantly agitated at 150 rpm at 37 °C. At a pre-determined time, intervals of 1, 2, 3, 4, 8, 10, 12, 24, 26, 48 and 72 h, 0.5 mL of buffer was withdrawn from the outer compartment of the device and fresh buffer was added in equal volume. It was then quantified by measuring absorbance at 254 nm. The cumulative concentration of the sulforaphane was measured from its dose-absorption curve.

c (t) = amount of drug released in supernatant.
c (o) = total amount of drug encapsulated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of CeNP-CD conjugated SFSNPs nanocomposites (CeNP-CD@SFSNPs)
ce:section.ce:para: UV Visible Spectroscopic analysis: All the absorption measurements at 200–800 nm were performed using Lasany UV–visible spectrophotometer LI-2800. Samples were dissolved in their respective solvents (such as water, DMSO and acetone) in specific concentrations and absorbance was measured using Quartz cuvette (path length: 10 mm).

Surface Charge and Hydrodynamic Size Measurement: Samples were dissolved in water and sonicated for 10 min and zeta potential and hydrodynamic size was measured using Malvern Nano ZS 90 Zetasizer operating at 25 °C.

XRD analysis: XRD spectra of the synthesized nanocomposites was acquired using X-Ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS D8 Advance) operating at (Cu-Kα radiation, λ = 1.54064 Å). The samples were coated on a glass slide by drop casting technique and analyzed in a range of 20–80° at a scan speed of 1°/min.
FTIR analysis: The FTIR spectrum was recorded by mixing the powder samples with KBr to make pellets and analyzed the spectrum using a Thermo Nicolet Spectrophotometer at a range of 4000–400 cmˉ1

Fluorescent spectroscopic analysis: The fluorescence emission pattern were acquired using a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried using Fluoro Cube Fluorescence Lifetime System (Horiba Jobin Yvon) equipped with a nano LED (635 nm) source. The decay curves were analyzed with IBH decay analysis v 6.1 software. For acquiring the fluorescence spectra, 10–20 μL (from stock solution) of the CD sample was dissolved in 1 mL of water. The fluorescence spectra of CeNP-CD conjugated SFSNPs nano-composites were also recorded with the equivalent concentration of CDs using fluorescence spectrophotometer for comparison. For, lifetime measurements, 50 μL of CDs and 150 μL of nanocomposite (∼CD content) were diluted to 5 mL with deionized water (final volume) and then subjected for analysis with fluorescence lifetime system consisted of a nano LED source (635 nm) source. The decay curves analysis was done with IBH decay analysis v 6.1 software.

Field Emission-Scanning Electron Microscopic (FE-SEM) analysis: The morphology of the SFSNPs were analyzed using after gold sputtering (Denton gold sputter unit for 90 s) using Ultra plus-Carl Zeiss FE-SEM operating at 5.0 kV.
Transmission Electron Microscopic (TEM) analysis: 20 μL of the sample solution was drop casted on carbon-coated copper grids and air dried for 6 h and was analyzed using FEI TECHNAI G2.

A549 (Human lung adenocarcinoma) and L132 (human lung epithelial) cells were obtained from the National Centre of Cell Sciences (NCCS) Pune, India. These cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagles Medium (DMEM) that was supplemented with 10% (v/v) FBS and 1% penicillin-streptomycin solution. They were maintained at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator under humidified conditions. When the cells reached 70–80% confluence, the subculture was done using 0.25% trypsin-EDTA.

A calorimetric MTT assay was used to assess the cellular viability. 1 × 104 cells/well (100 μL total volume/well) were seeded in a 96-well cell culture plate and were incubated for 24 h to allow attachment. Free sulforaphane (1.5,3,4.5,6 and 7.5 μg mL−1) and CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites (20,40,60 80 and 100 mg mL−1 with equivalent drug concentration) were added to fresh cell culture medium and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. CeNPs (5 mg mL−1), CDs (10 mg mL−1) and blank SFNPs (20,40,60,80 and 100 mg mL−1 with equivalent drug concentrations) were used as controls. After the treatment, washing of cells were done with PBS to remove the residual particles. The fresh medium (100 μL) containing 10 μL MTT (5 mg mL−1 in PBS) was added to each well and left for 4 h in 5% CO2 at 37 °C. After 4 h, the medium consisting of MTT was aspirated, 100 μL of DMSO was added to dissolve the formazan crystals. The absorbance of purple formazan product (570 nm) was recorded using multi-mode plate reader (Cytation 3, Biotek). All the experiments were performed in triplicates. Relative cell viability [mean (%) ± SEM, n = 3] was estimated as follows:

For cellular imaging, A549 cells (1 × 105 cells/mL) were seeded in 24-well culture plate and cultured overnight. Followed by the attachment of cells, free CDs,CeNPs, SFNPs, and CeNP-CD@SFSNPs (with equivalent concentration of sulforaphane 1.5,3,4.5,6 and 7.5 μg mL−1) were added to fresh DMEM medium in respective wells and left for incubation for 24 h at 37 °C. After treatment cells were washed with PBS twice, and their nucleus was stained with 2 μL of Hoechst 33324 dye (10 mg mL−1) for 10 min the, cells were also counter stained with 1 μL of lysotracker dye and incubated for 10 min. After that, the excessive dye was removed by washing with PBS. Cells were then analyzed using an inverted fluorescent microscope (EVOS FL Color, AMEFC 4300). For imaging, the filters used were DAPI (for Hoechst 33324, λex 360 nm, λem 447 nm), GFP (for CDs, λex 470 nm, λem 525 nm) and RFP (for lysotracker, λex 530 nm, λem 593 nm).

DCFH-DA staining. 2′, 7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) dye-based assay was carried out to determine the intracellular ROS levels. Initially, cells (1 × 105) were seeded on 6-well culture plates. Next day cells were incubated with increasing concentrations (20 μg/mL, 60 μg/mL and 100 μg/mL) of CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites (with equivalent concentration of sulforaphane 1.5,3,4.5,6 and 7.5 μg mL−1) for 24 h prior to the H2O2 treatment. Untreated cells with no sample were used as controls to determine the normal ROS levels in the cells. After that, the cells were washed with PBS and treated by exposing them to 750 μM H2O2 for 4 h. Cells were then suspended in PBS containing DCFH-DA dye (working concentration 20 mM) and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C for dye diffusion. Images were then acquired using GFP filter of the inverted fluorescence microscope. While quantitative decrease fluorescence intensity was recorded using multiplate reader at λex 488 nm, λem 535 nm.

Flow cytometry. For flow analysis, the above procedure was same and after that trypsinization was performed. The cell suspension was prepared by suspending cells in 200 μL PBS. The fluorescent intensity of the DCF (oxidized product) was measured using 488 nm excitation laser of the flow cytometer (Amnis Flow sight). Amnis ideas software was used to analyze 10000 gated events of each sample acquired in channel 2 and ROS generation was expressed in terms of percentage of cells that expressed green fluorescence.

Statistical analysis: All the experiments are conducted in triplicates (n = 3) The values are expressed as mean ± standard error mean (SEM) for all experiments. The data were analyzed via students t-test or two-way ANOVA, whichever was applicable, using GraphPad Prism 6.0 and statistically significant values are denoted by **(p < 0.01), ***(p < 0.0010, ****(p < 0.0001).

Sulforaphane loaded silk fibroin nanoparticles (SFSNPs) synthesis and characterizations. Silk Fibroin is a protein consisting of 5509 amino acids and comprising of heavy (390 kDa) and light (25 kDa) chains. Its heavy chain consists of 12 hydrophobic domains with glycine-X repeats and 46% glycine content. In solution, it exists in block copolymer confirmation with a hydrophobic core and hydrophilic shell [46,47]. Sulforaphane loaded nanoparticles were synthesized via desolvation method, using acetone as desolvating agent [48]. Sulforaphane being hydrophobic drug [49] gets entrapped in matrix due to self-derived cross-linking of hydrophobic chains of the silk fibroin. UV–visible absorption spectra of silk fibroin nanoparticles demonstrated a characteristic absorption peak of protein at 273 nm due to the presence of tyrosine [50]. While drug exhibited a wide range of absorption peaks from 200 to 260 nm. In drug loaded silk fibroin NPs wide range of absorption peaks were observed from 200 to 260 nm similar to drug alone which indicated its entrapment in silk fibroin matrix due to chemical cross-linking of hydrophobic chains of silk fibroin and formation of the protein-drug complex (Fig. 1 A). FTIR spectra revealed the chemical interactions between sulforaphane and silk fibroin during the formation of sulforaphane loaded silk fibroin nanoparticles (SFSNPs). Peaks at 1635 cm−1, 1535 cm−1 and 1520 cm−1 correspond to amide 1 (C=O) stretching vibration, amide 2 (N–H bending and C–H stretching) and amide 3 (C–N stretching and C=O bending) of silk fibroin in SFSNPs [48,51]. Presence of sulforaphane in SFSNPs was confirmed by the appearance of the characteristic bond at 2182 cm−1 and 2110 cm−1 that is related to N=C=S stretching vibration of sulforaphane. While peaks at 2958 cm−1 and 2892 cm−1 are linked to C–H bond in sulforaphane. Other peaks at 1462 and 1357 cm−1 corresponds to deformation vibration of C–H from CH3 and CH2 in sulforaphane. Another additional major peak at 1021 cm−1 relates to S=O bond of sulforaphane while at 3420 cm−1 is due to physically absorbed water molecules (Fig. 2 A) [52]. The SFSNPs were spherical in shape with no obvious aggregations based on SEM micrographs having an average diameter of about 69 ± 26.44 nm (Fig. 1C). The entrapment efficiency of sulforaphane was determined from the dose absorption curve using UV–Vis spectrophotometer and was found to be 65.21%.

DLS and zeta potential investigation was carried out to confirm the coupling of CeNPs and CDs on the surface of SFSNPs. DLS results demonstrated that SFSNPs have an average hydrodynamic size of 260 ± 28.5 nm while CeNPs and CDs have an average hydrodynamic size of 6.0 ± 3.5 nm and 5.2 ± 3.6 nm. The average hydrodynamic size of the particles gets increased to 365 ± 20.2 nm due to the coupling of CeNP-CD with the SFSNPs (Fig. S1). Similarly, the zeta potential of SFSNPs was found to be −32.8 mV whereas for CDs and CeNPs was +20.6 mV and +26.3 mV. It was reduced to −15.3 mV after the formation of CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites. The reduction in the zeta potential and increment in hydrodynamic diameter is attributed to the electrostatic interactions between negatively charged (anionic) SFSNPs and positively charged (cationic) CDs and CeNPs [53]. These results validated the formation of self-assembled CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites due to the grafting of CDs and CeNPs on the surface of SFSNPs (Fig. S1).

CeNPs were initially evaluated by UV–visible spectrophotometer and displayed a broad absorption peak at 330 nm (Fig. S2(A)). It is due to charge transfer transition from O2 −(2p) to Ce4+ (4f) orbitals 30 in CeNPs. FTIR spectra of CeNPs displayed peaks at 3431 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 which corresponds to physically absorbed water molecules on the surface of CeNPs, while the strong absorption peak around 2925 cm−1 is assigned to C–H stretching vibration. Major peaks at around 439 cm−1 refers to Ce–O stretching band which confirms the formation of CeNPs. Two another strong peaks at around 1538 cm−1 and 1455 cm−1 refers to the carboxylic group symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration present in acetic acid [54] (Fig. S2(B)). Carboxyl group of the acetic acid get chemically bound to the surface of synthesized CeNPs which imparts them with high colloidal stability and zeta potential.

Further to investigate the presence of CeNPs in CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites XRD analysis was done. Fig. 2 (B&C) represents the XRD spectra of CeNPs and comparative XRD spectra of CeNP-CD@SFSNPs. CeNPs have a cubic fluorite-type structure displaying characteristic peaks at 28.5°, 33.1°, 47.4°,56.3° corresponding to 111, 200, 220, 311 planes (Fig. 2B) (JCPDS 78–0694) [55]. Silk fibroin nanoparticles (SFNPs-blank) (without sulforaphane) has a semi-crystalline structure due to cross-linking of the β sheets as compared to lyophilized silk fibroin which has an amorphous structure. Peaks at 2θ = 20.38° and 24.58° corresponds to crystalline β-sheets in silk fibroin nanoparticles [51]. In CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites 3 major additional peaks were observed at 2θ = 28.5°, 7.5°, 56.3° which were attributed to 111, 220, 311 planes of CeNPs along with a major peak of silk fibroin at 2θ = 20.38°. This confirmed the grafting of CeNPs with SFSNPs due to electrostatic interactions. This data also predicts that there is a shift to a more crystalline structure (Fig. 2C).

TEM investigation was also carried out to determine the morphology and size of CeNPS, CDs and CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites. The as-synthesized CeNPs and CDs appeared spherical in nature with an average size varying from 5 nm to 8 nm (CeNPs) and 2 nm–5nm (CDs) (Fig. S3). TEM analysis demonstrated blank SFNPs and CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites have a quasi-spherical kind of morphology along with the black dots indicating the presence of CeNPs and CDs. (Fig. 1B). EDX analysis also confirmed the presence of cerium, sulphur, carbon, nitrogen and oxygen as major elements present in the nanocomposites. (Fig. S4).

In UV–visible absorption spectra of the CDs, the characteristic peaks at 288 nm and 352 nm were obtained which correspond to the π -π* and n- π* transition of C=C and C=O bonds in CDs [36](Fig. 3 A). Moreover, the aqueous solution of CDs displays molecular signature excitation-dependent emission behaviour. With increment in excitation wavelength from 320 nm to 500 nm, there is a concurrent decrease in emission intensity with maximum emission intensity shifting from 445 nm to 554 nm. This phenomenon is referred to as redshift of the emission intensity and maximum emission intensity was recorded at the absorption maximum at an excitation of 340 nm and 360 nm (Fig. 3B) [24,56,57],. CDs exhibited a good photostability with marginal decrease in fluorescence intensity (1.09%) under continuous irradiation for 2 h (Fig. S2(C)). Comparative fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs and CeNP-CD@SFSNPs were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 3C). After grafting of CDs and CeNPs on the surface of SFSNPs, fluorescence intensity was observed. The fluorescence intensity of CeNP-CD@SFSNPs is slightly lower than blank CDs and CD@SFSNPs which may be attributed to photoinduced electron transfer between CDs, CeNPs and SFSNPs components [45,58,59]. To confirm these results fluorescent lifetime decay curves for CDs, CD@SFSNPS, and CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites were recorded by fitting the obtained data to a triple-exponential decay curve and by calculating average lifetimes (Fig. 3D and Table S1).The average lifetime of CeNP-CD@SFSNPs was found to be 1.025 ns that was slightly less as compared to blank CDs and CD@SFSNPs.

The effect of change of pH on the fluorescence properties of CDs, CD@SFSNPs, CeNP-CD@SFSNPs was also determined (Fig. S5). The emission intensity CDs, CD@SFSNPs and CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites varied when the pH of the medium was changed. There was a steady increase in the fluorescence intensity at a pH from 4 to 7 with maximum fluorescence was observed at pH 7 in all the three cases. Shift in pH from acidic to basic causes a reduction in fluorescence with minimum fluorescence was recorded at pH 10. This pH-dependent behaviour is ascribed to the protonation and deprotonation of the amine groups present in CDs [36,60]. However, interestingly, an increase in fluorescence intensity was observed at a pH 5 in case of CeNP-CD@SFSNPs which may be due to the presence of CeNPs. pH dependent fluorescence suggests that at lysosomotropic pH, these composites will fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro release studies of sulforaphane
ce:section.ce:para: Another important parameter associated with the CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposite is to release sulforaphane in a sustained manner. pH controlled release of sulforaphane was administered in vitro at pH 4.5 and pH 7.4 respectively. Time-dependent release profile of sulforaphane is shown in Fig. 4 A and pH based stability of nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 4B. Sulforaphane was released in a biphasic system. Initially, there was a burst release (due to drug attached on the surface) followed by sustained release. More than 60% of the drug was released after 72 h at pH 4.5, imitating the lysosomotropic environment. While only 49% of the drug was released at pH 7.4 after 72 h. The entrapment of drug within the silk fibroin is derived majorly by hydrophobic forces. The rapid release in acidic conditions may be due to week interactions between the drug molecules and silk fibroin. Moreover, some amount of drug is also located at hydrophobic and hydrophilic interface inside the particles leading to its diffusion from the NPs [44,61,62]. These nanocomposites are more stable at neutral pH(7.0) as compared with acidic and basic pH on the basis of theri hydrodynamic size as shown in Fig. 4B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free radical scavenging activity of CDs and CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites using DPPH assay
ce:section.ce:para: DPPH assay was carried out to determine the free radical scavenging activity of CDs and CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites. DPPH is deep purple colour solution containing nitrogen-free radicals which turns yellow on interaction with antioxidant [24,63]. To 50 μM methanolic DPPH solution, different concentrations of CDs were added and a decrease in absorbance was determined after 30 min of incubation at 517 nm. The free radical scavenging activity was observed in a dose-dependent manner (Fig. S6). There was an increase in % scavenging activity from 15 to 70%, with the increase in the concentration of CDs from 5 to 100 μg/mL. From the curve, the EC50 value was estimated to be 70 μg/mL. It is the amount of antioxidant required to reduce the concentration of DPPH by 50%. DPPH solution turned from deep purple to colourless and then to yellow with increasing concentration of CDs. These results validated that CDs not only facilitated the molecular imaging but also enhanced the overall antioxidant potential of the nanocomposites. The antioxidant property demonstrated by CDs can be attributed to the presence of Quercetin in mulberry leaves. Quercetin is a potential antioxidant [64,65]. Similar kind of results was observed in case of CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites and EC50 value was determined to be 50 μg/mL (Fig. S6) However, the nanocomposites more efficiently reduced DPPH solution.

Furthermore, Cell Viability of the CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites was investigated in vitro using MTT assay (Fig. 5 A &B). A549 cells treated with blank SFNPs, CeNPs, CDs exhibited appreciable cell viability of CDs, CeNPs and SFNPs at all the tested concentrations. Free sulforaphane was found to significantly inhibit cell growth with increasing concentration (1.5,3,4.5,6 and 7.5 μg mL−1). The IC 50 concentration of the alone drug was found to be around 3.0 μg mL−1 and for nanocomposites found to be around 100 μg mL−1). The cell viability was reduced to nearly 24.711% at the highest concentration (7.5 μg mL−1). On contrary, CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites were comparatively non-toxic at concentrations(20,40,60 and 80 μg mL−1) with equivalent concentration of drug (1.5,3,4.5,6 μg mL−1) but Cell viability at the highest concentration(100 μg mL−1) was found to be around 57.59% (Fig. 5B). The reduction in cell viability at the highest concentration is attributed to the release of sulforaphane from the nanoparticles. In addition to the antioxidant activity via Nrf2 pathway sulforaphane also shows anticancer activity due to the induction of caspase 3 and p21 and downregulation of hsp90 molecular chaperone [66,67]. Effect of CeNP-CD@SFSNPs was also examined on normal lung epithelial cell line L132 (Fig. 5A). The more inhibitory effect was observed in A549 cells as compared to normal lung epithelial cells at the highest concentration of 100 μg mL−1. In addition to showing antioxidant properties, they also inhibit cancer cells in higher concentration of drug.

Fluorescence based imaging is an important tool to detect the uptake of fluorescent nanomaterials (such as CDs) by the cells Fig. 6 shows the fluorescence microscopic images of A549 cells incubated with CDs, CeNPs, SFNPs and CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposite under similar conditions. CDs were emitts green color fluorescene under the blue excitation wavelength (Fig. 6c). The location of free CeNPs and SFNPs in A549 cells after internalization was traced by treating the cells with lysotracker red which emits red fluorescence under green excitation wavelength (Fig. 6i and 6n) for imaging of lysosomes. To visualize the nucleus, the cells were stained with Hoechst 33342 which emits blue color under UV excitation wavelength. After 24 h of incubation, CDs, CeNPS and SFNPs were mostly uptaken by the cell into its cytoplasm but could not transported to the nucleus (Fig. 6e).This interprets the cytoplasm of the cells are the main cellular target of blank CDs, CeNPs and SFNPs [57,67,68]. Moreover, CeNP-CD@SFSNPs was also traced in cytoplasmic region surrounding the nucleus and showed colocalization of green and red fluorescence due to the release of CDs, CeNPs and SFNPs, respectively; (Fig. 6q-t). After incubation, the green color fluorescence of nanocompositesconfirms its presence within the cells which concur with the lysotracker red fluorescence. This demonstrated that CeNPs-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites accumulated sufficiently in the cytoplasm of cells after internalization and later moved to the lysosomes. These results confirms that the lysosomes are the target organelles of CeNPs-CD@SFSNPs. These results demonstrate the significance of Fluorescent CDs as tracers for delivery of therapeutic agents [68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant studies and flow cytometry analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Further, the ability of CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites to scavenge ROS from H2O2 mediated oxidative stress was analyzed by fluorescence-based DCFH-DA assay. DCFH-DA is a non-fluorescent compound that gets oxidized to highly fluorescent dichlorofluorescein (DCF) due to the action of esterases present inside the cells, upon cellular uptake which forms the basis of ROS detection. Fluorescent microscopic images were taken by exposing A549 cells to increasing concentrations of CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites (Fig. 7 A). Fig. 7B shows the quantitative decrease in fluorescence intensity of the DCF at λex 488 nm, λem 535 nm. The high intensity of green fluorescence was observed in the cells treated with H2O2 compared to untreated cells. While the green fluorescence signal subsequently decreased in the cells exposed to the varying concentrations of CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites. This is due to the ROS scavenging effect mediated by nanocomposites implying that the nanocomposites can efficiently reduce ROS levels resulted from oxidative stress [45,55].

Further, ROS scavenging activity was quantitatively analyzed by flow cytometry. It was reported in terms of percentage number of gated cells expressing DCF signal with respect to the control cells (Fig. 7C). There was a shift in fluorescence that was observed from the region of higher oxidative stress to the lower oxidative stress. Fluorescent signal of treated cells was less as compared to cells exposed to H2O2. Percentage of ROS levels decreased from 36.4% to 8.1% with increasing concentrations of the CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites as compared to untreated cells. Cells without H2O2 treatment were kept as control. However, it is important to mention that CDs have a relatively very week green fluorescent signal as compared to commercially available dyes, so they did not interfere with the flow measurements [68]. Nanocomposites not only decreased ROS but also were efficient in scavenging hydrogen peroxide radicals mediated from H2O2 stress.

CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites are internalized by cells in the cytoplasm and transported to lysosome through endocytosis validated using intracelluar co-localization imaging as shown in Fig. S7. In lysosome, due to action of lysosomal enzymes and low pH, (acidic nature) CDs and CeNPs (as validated by aqueous stability in Fig. 4B) dissociate from nanocomposites and sulforaphane gets released from silk fibroin nanoparticles. They get distributed into the cell. Sulforaphane activates Antioxidant Response Element (ARE) via nrf2 pathway in nucleus. ARE is responsible for expression of various antioxidant genes in nucleus [66,67].

Cerium oxide nanoparticles further enhances the removal of ROS due to their mixed valency i.e. reduced Ce3+ and oxidized Ce4+ state [69]. While carbon dots (CDs) enable molecular imaging exhibiting green fluorescence in addition to enhancement of overall antioxidant potential of CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites.

Herein, we report CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites for scavenging harmful reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as H2O2 by simultaneously enabling the intracellular imaging of the cells. A single step feasible and reproducible method has been used to incorporate sulforaphane (antioxidant drug) in silk fibroin nanoparticles. While bPEI-passivated carbon dots (CDs) and cerium oxide nanoparticles were synthesized using the hydrothermal method. The formation of nanocomposites involves around the electrostatic interactions between negatively charged sulforaphane loaded silk fibroin nanoparticles and positively charged CeNPs and bPEI-Passivated (positive charge) CDs. Various cell biological assays confirm the suitability of CeNP-CD@SFSNPs nanocomposites to scavenge ROS in H2O2 induced oxidative stress in lung cancer cell line. The rationale behind efficient removal of ROS is Nrf2 pathway activation by sulforaphane. SOD and catalase mimetic activity of cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeNPs) due to mixed valency that further augmented antioxidant potential. In addition to molecular imaging, CDs also enhanced antioxidant activity. In a nutshell, the present work prompts the use of multifunctional CeNP-CD@SFSNPs for imaging and therapy for various pulmonary disorders such as COPD, and other disorders due to oxidative stress. Further, studies on surface functionalization of the nanocomposites are underway to attain targeted therapy.
